destination.name: Rheingau
destination.hero_image_credit: Photo by Joshua Kettle on Unsplash
destination.hero_image_alt: Rheingau wine country, aerial view of the Rhine bend with Riesling slopes and a half-timbered river town
destination.blurb: The Rhine bend west of Wiesbaden where Riesling has been the singular grape since the 1720 monastic replanting at Schloss Johannisberg and Spätburgunder defines Assmannshausen.
destination.tagline: Riesling on quartzite and loess from Hochheim to Lorch, Spätburgunder on red slate at Assmannshausen
destination.overview: The Rheingau follows roughly 50 km of the Rhine from Hochheim am Main in the east to Lorchhausen in the west, where the river makes its great south-facing bend before turning north at Bingen. Vineyards face south on quartzite, loess and slate soils, sheltered by the Taunus hills. Riesling accounts for about 78 percent of plantings, and the Hessian state monastery domain at Kloster Eberbach is documented as a Cistercian foundation of 1135 and as the cradle of the modern VDP. The 2002 Grosses Gewächs (GG) framework for dry top-vineyard wines originated here, and the Assmannshausen enclave on red slate is the historic German home of Spätburgunder. The region also contains the Hochschule Geisenheim wine university.

index.title: Rheingau Wine Region Guide | Cork & Curve
index.description: Rheingau guide: Riesling estates from Hochheim to Lorch, Spätburgunder at Assmannshausen, plus Schloss Johannisberg and Kloster Eberbach visits.

vineyards.title: Rheingau Vineyards and Estates | Cork & Curve
vineyards.description: Rheingau Weinguter from Schloss Johannisberg and Kloster Eberbach to Robert Weil, Georg Breuer, Künstler and Leitz, with 60+ VDP estates listed.

wines.title: Rheingau Wines Catalog | Cork & Curve
wines.description: Rheingau cuvees from Hochheimer Hölle and Erbacher Marcobrunn to Rüdesheimer Berg Schlossberg and Assmannshausen Höllenberg, with taste profiles.

tasting-rooms.title: Rheingau Tasting Rooms | Cork & Curve
tasting-rooms.description: Rheingau Vinotheken and cellar doors worth booking, from estate tasting rooms in Hattenheim and Oestrich-Winkel to Hochheim and Rüdesheim wine bars.

wine-bars.title: Rheingau Wine Bars and Vinotheken | Cork & Curve
wine-bars.description: Riesling-focused wine bars and Vinotheken across Eltville and Rüdesheim pouring Grosses Gewächs, Erstes Gewächs and Prädikat wines by the glass.

wine-restaurants.title: Rheingau Wine Restaurants | Cork & Curve
wine-restaurants.description: Rheingau restaurants with serious Riesling cellars, from Schloss Johannisberg and Kloster Eberbach kitchens to Hochheim and Eltville dining rooms.

signature-wines.title: Iconic Rheingau Wines | Cork & Curve
signature-wines.description: Bottles that define the Rheingau, from Robert Weil Gräfenberg and Künstler Hochheimer Hölle to Schloss Johannisberg Silberlack and Höllenberg Pinot.

signature-grapes.title: Rheingau Signature Grapes | Cork & Curve
signature-grapes.description: Rheingau grape varieties, led by Riesling on quartzite and loess, with Spätburgunder on Assmannshausen red slate, plus the Burgunder family.

neighborhoods.title: Rheingau Sub-Regions and Wine Villages | Cork & Curve
neighborhoods.description: Rheingau villages from Hochheim and Walluf east to Eltville, Erbach, Hattenheim, Kiedrich, Oestrich-Winkel, Johannisberg, Rüdesheim and Lorch.

faqs.q: What is the Rheingau wine region known for?
faqs.a: The Rheingau is the south-facing Rhine bend west of Wiesbaden where Riesling accounts for around 78 percent of plantings. Schloss Johannisberg documents a 1720 monastic replanting with Riesling alone, the 1775 'Spätlese' courier story is recorded by the same estate, and the dry VDP.Grosses Gewächs (GG) framework launched here in 2002. Assmannshausen, at the western end, is the historic German home of Spätburgunder on red slate.

faqs.q: When is the best time to visit the Rheingau?
faqs.a: Late April to early June for vine flowering and the Rheingau Musik Festival programme at Kloster Eberbach, late August to early October for harvest and the Rheingauer Weinwoche in Wiesbaden, and late October for Federweisser. Winter visits revolve around the Glühwein huts of Rüdesheim and tastings at the estate Vinotheken.

faqs.q: How are Rheingau Rieslings labelled, dry versus fruity-sweet?
faqs.a: Rheingau Rieslings carry two parallel labels. Dry wines from top single vineyards are labelled Grosses Gewächs (GG), the dry VDP.Grosse Lage tier from 2002 onwards. Fruity-sweet wines still use the legal Prädikat ladder: Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese and Eiswein, with sweetness rising up the ladder. A village name on its own (e.g. Hochheimer Hölle Kabinett) signals the Prädikat track.

faqs.q: What is the difference between VDP.Grosse Lage and a legal Prädikat?
faqs.a: VDP.Grosse Lage is the top tier of the private Verband Deutscher Prädikatsweinguter site classification, marking the most prized single vineyards. The legal Prädikat (Kabinett, Spätlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese, Trockenbeerenauslese, Eiswein) is a separate state-regulated ripeness ladder applied at harvest. A single wine can carry both: a 'Rauenthaler Baiken Riesling Kabinett' from a VDP estate is Prädikat Kabinett grown on a VDP.Grosse Lage site.

faqs.q: Why is Hochheim wine called 'hock' in English?
faqs.a: Hochheim am Main is the easternmost Rheingau commune. From the early 19th century, British importers used 'hock' as a generic English shorthand for Rhine wine, derived from Hochheim. The Hochheimer Königin Victoriaberg vineyard, named after Queen Victoria's 1850 visit, supplied the British royal household for decades and cemented the association.

faqs.q: Where does the Rheingau grow Spätburgunder?
faqs.a: The historic German Spätburgunder enclave is at Assmannshausen on the steep red slate slopes of the Assmannshäuser Höllenberg, just downstream of Rüdesheim. Estates working the site include Weingut Krone Assmannshausen, Weingut August Kesseler and the Hessische Staatsweinguter's Domäne Assmannshausen. Smaller Spätburgunder plots also dot the central Rheingau.

Yes. Kloster Eberbach is a 1135-founded Cistercian monastery near Eltville, used as a film location for The Name of the Rose. The Hessische Staatsweinguter Kloster Eberbach runs daily tours from 10am, a tasting Vinothek and seasonal wine events. The monastery basilica and the lay refectory are open without a winery booking.

faqs.q: What day trips work from a Rheingau base?
faqs.a: Wiesbaden (10 km from Eltville) for spa-town dining; Mainz (across the Rhine) for the cathedral and the Frankfurt-Mainz wine bar circuit; Frankfurt am Main (40 km from Hochheim) for the airport-side urban anchor; the Mittelrhein UNESCO valley with Bacharach and the Loreley downstream from Lorch; and the Niederwald monument above Rüdesheim, reached by cable car from the town centre.

faqs.q: Is the Niederwald monument worth the cable car?
faqs.a: Yes, especially for the vineyard view. The Niederwalddenkmal is a 38-metre Germania statue inaugurated in 1883 above the Rüdesheimer Berg, accessible by the Seilbahn cable car from Oberstrasse in Rüdesheim. The terrace overlooks the Berg Schlossberg, Berg Rottland and Berg Roseneck vineyards and the bend of the Rhine towards Bingen.

The Rheingauer Riesling-Route is a 70-kilometre signposted driving and cycling route from Flörsheim-Wicker in the east through Hochheim, Eltville, Hattenheim, Oestrich-Winkel, Johannisberg, Rüdesheim and Assmannshausen to Lorchhausen in the west. It links the region's main wine villages, Kloster Eberbach, Schloss Johannisberg and the major Vinotheken.
